title: Lefschetz Without Positivity: An Overview
speaker: Karim Alexander Adiprasito
affiliation: Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: The Lefschetz property is central in the theory of projective varieties, detailing a fundamental property of their Chow rings, essentially saying that the multiplication with a geometrically motivated class is of full rank.

proof_stages.label: Density problem for embedded complexes
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the classical planar graph edge bound and formulates its higher-dimensional analogue: a k-dimensional simplicial complex PL-embedded in R^{2k} should have its number of k-faces bounded by a constant times its number of (k-1)-faces.

proof_stages.label: Translation to conewise-polynomial cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The embedded complex is placed inside a triangulated sphere or fan, the conewise-polynomial algebra P(sigma) and its Artinian quotient A(sigma) are introduced, and face-number estimates are converted into the target inequality dim A_{k+1}(Delta) <= dim A_k(Delta).

proof_stages.label: Lefschetz isomorphism as the missing comparison
proof_stages.summary: Poincare duality for the ambient fan gives equality of complementary dimensions but does not descend to the subcomplex, so the required inequality would follow from multiplication by a degree-one Lefschetz element, as in the classical Hard Lefschetz and Hodge-Riemann theorem for strictly convex classes.

proof_stages.label: Generic Lefschetz without convexity for homology spheres
proof_stages.summary: Because many homology spheres or positive-characteristic settings have no meaningful strictly convex class, the main theorem replaces positivity by generic choices of parameters theta and l, yielding Poincare duality, Hard Lefschetz, nondegenerate Hodge-Lefschetz pairings on squarefree monomial ideals, and in characteristic two a stronger total-anisotropy statement.

proof_stages.label: Self-pairing criterion and linear-algebra induction
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is sketched: first establish that every nonzero element has nonzero self-product, then use a linear-algebra lemma about generically combining maps to iteratively shrink kernels and construct Lefschetz isomorphisms from the nondegeneracy of the relevant pairings.

proof_stages.label: Extension to semigroup algebras of lattice polytopes
proof_stages.summary: The same philosophy is applied beyond simplicial complexes to standard Gorenstein semigroup algebras of lattice polytopes, where generic Hard Lefschetz in characteristic two or zero explains the observed unimodality of Ehrhart-type h-vectors.

proof_stages.label: Reduced Gorenstein conjecture and genericity clarifications
proof_stages.summary: The talk culminates in a conjectural dream that any reduced standard graded Gorenstein algebra of positive codimension should have generic Artinian reductions satisfying Hard Lefschetz and total anisotropy, followed by discussion clarifying non-generic failures, the difference from positivity, field-extension issues, and the lack of known applications requiring a specific Lefschetz element.

examples.name: Planar graph edge bound
examples.note: The Euler double-counting bound for a planar graph illustrates the kind of density obstruction that the later higher-dimensional face-number theorem generalizes.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives the planar edge bound by triangulating regions, double-counting edge-triangle incidences, and using Euler’s formula.

passes.reason: The planar bound is presented via maximal triangulated planar graphs attaining the optimal edge-density limit, as motivation rather than refutation.

examples.name: Different toric surface realizations of the same fan combinatorics
examples.note: Different drawings of the same combinatorial fan, such as realizations leading to P^1 x P^1 versus a Hirzebruch-type toric surface, make concrete why P(sigma) is combinatorial while the Artinian quotient A depends on the chosen linear system theta.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker describes different drawings/geometrizations of the same fan combinatorics and states that they give the same P(sigma) but different A(sigma), without carrying out a calculation on a specific pair.

passes.reason: Different toric surface realizations with the same fan combinatorics are used as a standard illustration that the Artinian quotient depends on the geometric realization.
The toric surface realizations are ordinary members used to illustrate dependence on the chosen geometrization.
The paired toric surface realizations are standard examples used to show that the Artinian quotient is not determined by fan combinatorics alone.
Different toric surface realizations of the same fan combinatorics are used to illustrate dependence on geometrization, not as structurally sporadic or adversarial objects.

The different toric surface drawings illustrate ordinary variation of the geometric realization theta while keeping the same combinatorial P(sigma), with no refutation intended.

examples.name: P^1 x P^1 ray divisor as a non-generic failure
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker uses a ray divisor on the fan of P^1 x P^1 whose square dies modulo global linear functions to show that positivity does not imply the self-pairing or total-anisotropy property and that genericity is necessary.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly analyzes the P^1 x P^1 ray divisor, writes the associated conewise-linear function, squares it, identifies it with a product involving a global linear function, and concludes the self-product property fails.

passes.reason: A torus-invariant divisor on P^1 x P^1 lies on a boundary ray with self-intersection zero and is explicitly offered as a non-generic counterexample to the self-product/anisotropy property.
The torus-invariant fiber divisor on the symmetric P^1 x P^1 fan lies on a cone boundary and has square zero, giving the non-generic failure.
The chosen ray divisor on the highly regular P^1 x P^1 fan is a boundary square-zero class, giving the requested non-generic failure of self-pairing.
